title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
ALERRT Center · Texas State University




Cancer. Heart disease. PTSD. First responders face these at higher rates than the general population — but getting workers’ compensation often means proving your job caused it. Presumptive laws shift that burden, treating these illnesses as work-related by default. Coverage varies widely across all 50 states.

Open the Coverage Map →

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
What is a presumptive law?
Under standard workers’ compensation rules, a sick first responder must demonstrate that their specific job directly caused their illness — a nearly impossible bar for diseases like cancer or heart disease that develop over years. A presumptive law eliminates that requirement: the illness is presumed to be work-related, and the burden falls on the employer to prove otherwise.
Most presumptions are rebuttable — the employer can present contradicting evidence. A small number are irrebuttable, meaning the link is conclusive regardless of other evidence. Coverage also depends on which conditions are listed in the statute, which occupations qualify, how many years of service are required, and whether there’s a post-retirement window.

Keeping track of where these protections exist — and what they actually cover — is harder than it should be. The Coverage Map is an attempt to fix that.

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
How to use this app


01 Pick a mode. Use Occupation to ask which conditions are covered for a specific responder type. Use Condition to ask which states cover a specific illness — and for whom.


02 Read the chart. The intersection diagram shows how many states cover each combination of responder groups. Hover a bar to see which states; click to lock the selection.


03 Click the map. Selecting a state opens a detailed table of all laws on record — statute citations, service requirements, post-employment coverage, and notes.


04 Verify before acting. Laws change. Always confirm current status with a qualified attorney or your state’s workers’ compensation board.

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
About this project
The Coverage Map was developed by the ALERRT Center at Texas State University. ALERRT conducts applied research on first responder training, health, and wellness.
The underlying data come from the IAFF Presumptive Health database, maintained by the International Association of Fire Fighters. We’ve structured and mapped that information to make it easier to search and compare — but the IAFF pages remain the authoritative source. See the About page for full methodology and citation.
Prototype This is an early-stage research tool. If you notice an error or a law that has changed, the About page explains how to interpret the data.

title: Coverage Map
text:
First Responder Presumptive Laws — Coverage Map

First visit? This app runs entirely in your browser — it may take up to a minute to initialize while R packages load in the background.

title: About
text:
Primary source IAFF Presumptive Health database — iaff.org/presumptive-health
Maintained by the International Association of Fire Fighters, this database covers presumptive workers’ compensation laws for first responders in all 50 states. Each state page includes statute citations, covered conditions, covered occupations, and links to the underlying law. This dashboard is essentially a structured, map-based interface for the information the IAFF has compiled. We have not independently verified the legal text of each statute.


Cross-check reference Neugent, K., & Spidell, B. (2023). Firefighters and first responders: 2023 update on presumptive workers comp benefits. National Council on Compensation Insurance (NCCI).
Covers approximately 38 NCCI-jurisdiction states; data current through November 2022. Used to cross-check IAFF data for covered states during initial extraction.


Cross-check reference Brandt-Rauf, S., Davis, A. L., & Taylor, J. A. (2024). Inventory of state workers’ compensation laws in the United States: first responder mental health. Journal of Public Health Policy, 45, 562–574.
50-state inventory using the Westlaw legal database; data current through December 2022. Used to supplement IAFF data for mental health / PTSD coverage, which is less consistently documented in other sources.

title: About
text:
Methodology
For each state, IAFF source pages were reviewed and law attributes — condition covered, occupations covered, statute citation, service requirements, post-employment coverage, and restrictions — were coded into a structured dataset. Each row represents one state × condition category × occupation combination.
Where the IAFF source characterized a provision as historical or inactive, that status is reflected in the data. A small number of entries are flagged with * in the Notes column where the active status of a statute is uncertain.

Data currency: The IAFF database is actively maintained, but this dashboard reflects a point-in-time extraction. Laws change. Always verify current status with a qualified attorney or your state’s workers’ compensation board before relying on this resource for legal or financial decisions.

title: About
text:
About ALERRT
This resource was developed by the ALERRT Center at Texas State University. ALERRT conducts research on first responder training, health, and wellness, with a focus on improving outcomes for first responders and the communities they serve.

text:
